stem: You are the obstetric registrar called urgently to the birth suite. A 38-year-old multiparous woman at 39+5 weeks has just delivered her third child. Immediately after delivery of the placenta she became acutely breathless, hypotensive, and developed a tonic-clonic seizure. The seizure has terminated after 30 seconds but she is now poorly responsive and has copious oozing from the episiotomy site and intravenous puncture sites. The blood loss is approximately 1500 mL and ongoing. Cardiac arrest team has been called. No fluid bolus has yet been given.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138 bpm, BP 72/40 mmHg, RR 38, SpO2 84% room air, Temp 36.7 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Drowsy GCS 10, central cyanosis, oozing from venepuncture sites, soft uterus, ongoing PV bleeding
data_table.Investigations: Point-of-care Hb 88 g/L, INR > 4.0, platelets 78 x10^9/L, fibrinogen 0.8 g/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management priority?

options.text: Commence intravenous magnesium sulfate 4 g loading dose for eclampsia and continue at 1 g per hour
options.rationale: Eclampsia is unlikely given the absence of antecedent hypertension or proteinuria and the dominant cardiovascular collapse and DIC. Magnesium will not treat the embolism, hypotension, or coagulopathy. (Source: ANZICS Statement - Amniotic Fluid Embolism)

options.text: Administer intramuscular adrenaline 500 micrograms for presumed anaphylactoid reaction to oxytocin
options.rationale: Although AFE has been described as anaphylactoid syndrome of pregnancy, intramuscular adrenaline alone is insufficient for the cardiovascular collapse, hypoxia, and DIC pattern seen. The priority is full ABCD resuscitation and MTP activation. (Source: ANZICS Statement - Amniotic Fluid Embolism)

options.text: Activate massive transfusion protocol while supporting airway, breathing and circulation with fluids and uterotonics
options.rationale: Amniotic fluid embolism presents with cardiovascular collapse, hypoxia, coagulopathy, and seizures in the peripartum period. Activation of the massive transfusion protocol with packed cells, FFP, cryoprecipitate, and platelets is the critical early intervention along with ABC support and uterotonics for uterine atony. Definitive treatment is supportive while DIC and hypovolaemia are corrected. (Source: ANZICS Statement - Amniotic Fluid Embolism)

options.text: Administer intravenous tissue plasminogen activator for presumed pulmonary thromboembolism
options.rationale: Thrombolysis in a woman with massive ongoing haemorrhage and severe DIC is contraindicated. Even if pulmonary embolism were considered, the bleeding contraindicates lysis. (Source: ANZICS Statement - Amniotic Fluid Embolism)

options.text: Defer transfusion until laboratory-confirmed coagulation results return and place urgent type-and-cross-match
options.rationale: Waiting for laboratory confirmation in a woman who is exsanguinating with point-of-care INR over 4 will be fatal. MTP activates blood-bank delivery of empirical balanced products immediately. (Source: ANZICS Statement - Amniotic Fluid Embolism)

explanation.summary: Amniotic fluid embolism is a rare but catastrophic obstetric emergency with maternal mortality of 20 to 60%. The classical triad is sudden cardiovascular collapse, hypoxia, and disseminated intravascular coagulation occurring peripartum, often with seizures. Management is supportive: high-quality CPR if arrest, advanced airway, vasopressors, and aggressive correction of coagulopathy via massive transfusion protocol. Uterotonics treat coexistent atony. ECMO has been used in refractory cases at tertiary centres. Early multidisciplinary involvement (obstetrics, anaesthesia, ICU, haematology) is essential.
key_points: Classical triad: cardiovascular collapse, hypoxia, DIC in the peripartum window
Activate massive transfusion protocol urgently; do not wait for lab confirmation
Uterotonics for coexistent atony; coagulation correction with FFP, cryoprecipitate, platelets
ECMO at tertiary centres in refractory cardiopulmonary failure
explanation.pearls: In suspected amniotic fluid embolism, fibrinogen should be replaced aggressively if less than 2 g/L using cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate - hypofibrinogenaemia is the strongest early predictor of ongoing haemorrhage and maternal death.

sources.label: National Blood Authority - Patient Blood Management Module 5: Obstetrics

stem: You are a GP registrar reviewing a 47-year-old woman who has been on lithium carbonate 1000 mg daily for bipolar I disorder for 4 years. Her mood has been stable on this dose with no relapses. She presents for an annual review and reports fatigue, mild constipation, modest weight gain, and dry skin over the past 6 months. She has no tremor, no polyuria, and no neurological symptoms. Her psychiatrist last reviewed her 6 months ago and was happy with her mood state. Her most recent lithium level 3 months ago was 0.7 mmol/L. The annual monitoring bloods have just been performed.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/76 mmHg, HR 64 bpm, BMI 28 kg/m2
data_table.Examination: No goitre, no tremor, no peripheral oedema, slow ankle jerk relaxation
data_table.Investigations: TSH 18.2 mIU/L, free T4 9 pmol/L, creatinine 78 micromol/L, eGFR 78 mL/min/1.73m2, calcium corrected 2.42 mmol/L, lithium level 0.7 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Cease lithium immediately and substitute sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily as mood stabiliser
options.rationale: Abrupt lithium cessation is associated with high rates of bipolar relapse, especially in a stable patient. Lithium-induced hypothyroidism is reversible with thyroxine while continuing lithium. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines - Mood Disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines - Mood Disorders

options.text: Commence levothyroxine 50 micrograms orally daily and continue lithium at current dose
options.rationale: Lithium-induced hypothyroidism affects up to 20% of patients on long-term lithium and is managed with levothyroxine replacement rather than lithium cessation, given the lithium is providing effective mood stabilisation. Starting dose of 50 micrograms is appropriate in a woman without cardiovascular disease. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines - Mood Disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines - Mood Disorders

options.text: Reduce lithium to 500 mg daily to allow thyroid recovery and continue close monitoring of mood
options.rationale: Subtherapeutic lithium risks bipolar relapse without reversing the autoimmune component of lithium-induced hypothyroidism, which is more reliably treated with thyroxine. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines - Mood Disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines - Mood Disorders

options.text: Recheck thyroid function in 6 weeks before considering replacement therapy in this asymptomatic phase
options.rationale: She has clear clinical hypothyroidism with fatigue, constipation, and dry skin alongside a TSH of 18 and low T4. Delaying replacement worsens symptoms unnecessarily. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines - Mood Disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines - Mood Disorders

options.text: Refer to endocrinology for thyroid autoantibodies and ultrasound before starting any treatment
options.rationale: Thyroid autoantibodies and imaging do not change the management of overt hypothyroidism. Thyroxine should commence promptly with monitoring through general practice. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines - Mood Disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines - Mood Disorders

explanation.summary: Lithium causes thyroid dysfunction in around 15 to 20% of patients on long-term therapy, predominantly hypothyroidism, and a smaller minority develop hyperparathyroidism. Australian best practice is to monitor TSH, calcium, creatinine, and lithium level every 6 to 12 months. When lithium-induced hypothyroidism develops in a patient with sustained mood stability, levothyroxine replacement is preferred over lithium cessation. Renal monitoring is for nephrogenic diabetes insipidus and chronic interstitial nephritis; baseline and annual eGFR are standard.
key_points: Lithium-induced hypothyroidism affects approximately 15 to 20% of long-term patients
Manage with thyroxine; do not stop lithium if mood stability has been hard-won
Annual monitoring: TSH, calcium, creatinine, lithium level, weight
Lithium also causes nephrogenic diabetes insipidus and chronic interstitial nephritis
explanation.pearls: In a patient stable on lithium who develops hypothyroidism, add levothyroxine rather than stopping lithium - bipolar relapse risk after lithium cessation is approximately 50% within 12 months, far exceeding the morbidity of treated hypothyroidism.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines - Mood Disorders

stem: A 52-year-old man attends the general practice where you work as a registrar. He has smoked 25 cigarettes per day for 30 years and wants to quit. He has tried unsuccessfully twice in the past with patches alone. He has stable type 2 diabetes and well-controlled hypertension. He has no history of mental illness, no seizure disorder, and is not currently depressed. He drinks 4 standard drinks per week. He has no contraindications and would prefer the most effective option, accepting some side effects. He wants to set a quit date in 2 weeks.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 134/82 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, BMI 29 kg/m2
data_table.Examination: Heart and chest unremarkable, no respiratory wheeze, peripheral pulses normal
data_table.Investigations: HbA1c 6.8%, eGFR 78 mL/min/1.73m2, no cardiovascular events documented in past 12 months

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Switch to a vape device with 18 mg/mL nicotine refill liquid and gradually reduce the strength
options.rationale: Australian regulation requires a prescription for nicotine vape and they are not first-line. They should be considered only after first-line therapies have failed or are unacceptable, with shared decision-making about evidence gaps. (Source: RACGP - Supporting Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Continue with single-modality nicotine replacement patch 21 mg daily for a further 12 weeks
options.rationale: Two prior failures with single-modality patch monotherapy predict another failure. Combination NRT (patch plus short-acting) or varenicline are more effective options. (Source: RACGP - Supporting Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Bupropion sustained-release 150 mg orally daily for 3 days then 300 mg daily for 12 weeks
options.rationale: Bupropion is a reasonable alternative when varenicline is contraindicated but has lower abstinence rates (around 19%) than varenicline (around 26%) and carries seizure and cardiovascular safety considerations. (Source: RACGP - Supporting Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Varenicline 0.5 mg orally daily for 3 days, then 0.5 mg twice daily for 4 days, then 1 mg twice daily
options.rationale: Varenicline has the highest efficacy of any single smoking cessation pharmacotherapy with continuous abstinence rates around 26% at 6 months. RACGP supports varenicline as a first-line option in adults without contraindications who have failed nicotine replacement. Quit date is set 1 to 2 weeks after starting. (Source: RACGP - Supporting Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Refer for hypnotherapy or acupuncture as a primary intervention with motivational interviewing
options.rationale: Behavioural therapies including motivational interviewing are useful adjuncts but as monotherapy they are less effective than first-line pharmacotherapy. Hypnotherapy and acupuncture have no robust evidence base. (Source: RACGP - Supporting Smoking Cessation)

explanation.summary: Pharmacotherapy combined with behavioural support roughly doubles smoking cessation success. Australian first-line options for adults without contraindications are varenicline, combination nicotine replacement therapy (patch plus short-acting form), and bupropion, with varenicline showing the highest single-agent efficacy. After two failures with NRT monotherapy, escalation to varenicline or combination NRT is rational. Vaping nicotine requires a prescription and is not first-line. Behavioural support via Quitline or Quit Coach raises success rates further.
key_points: Varenicline (around 26%) > combination NRT (around 22%) > bupropion (around 19%) at 6-month abstinence
Set quit date 1 to 2 weeks after starting varenicline
Bupropion contraindicated with seizure disorders and bulimia
Behavioural support roughly doubles efficacy of any pharmacotherapy
explanation.pearls: The EAGLES trial demonstrated that varenicline has comparable psychiatric safety to nicotine replacement therapy in patients with and without stable mental illness, removing the previous TGA boxed warning about neuropsychiatric adverse events.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Tobacco Use Disorder

stem: You are the psychiatry registrar called to review a 24-year-old man with a first episode of psychosis admitted 5 days ago. He was started on olanzapine 10 mg daily 3 days ago. Today he is mute, staring, and immobile. The nurse reports he has not eaten or drunk since this morning and has held his arm in an awkward position for over an hour without complaint. There is no fever and no rigidity. He has no past medical history and takes no other medications. His vital signs are stable. The medical registrar saw him this morning and excluded delirium with a normal collateral history of acute onset and stable medical workup.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 88 bpm, BP 122/78 mmHg, Temp 37.0 degrees C, RR 16, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Mute, immobile, posturing, waxy flexibility, no tremor, no rigidity, no clonus
data_table.Investigations: Creatine kinase 180 U/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, glucose 5.2 mmol/L, urinalysis clear, urine drug screen negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer haloperidol 5 mg intramuscularly for behavioural disturbance and continue olanzapine
options.rationale: Haloperidol can precipitate or worsen catatonia and may cause neuroleptic malignant syndrome in this state. It is not appropriate as a catatonia treatment or diagnostic test. (Source: RANZCP Guideline - Catatonia)

options.text: Increase olanzapine to 20 mg daily for presumed exacerbation of underlying psychotic illness
options.rationale: Antipsychotics can worsen catatonia and precipitate neuroleptic malignant syndrome, particularly in malignant catatonia. The first step is benzodiazepine challenge, with cautious antipsychotic dose review thereafter. (Source: RANZCP Guideline - Catatonia)

options.text: Cease olanzapine and commence intravenous dantrolene 2 mg/kg as muscle relaxant for rigidity
options.rationale: Dantrolene treats malignant hyperthermia and may be used adjunctively in severe NMS, but is not indicated in catatonia without hyperthermia or marked rigidity. The pathognomonic finding here is waxy flexibility, not rigidity. (Source: RANZCP Guideline - Catatonia)

options.text: Administer lorazepam 2 mg intramuscularly and reassess catatonic features at 30 to 60 minutes
options.rationale: The lorazepam challenge is both diagnostic and therapeutic for catatonia. A response within 30 to 60 minutes (loosening of posturing, return of speech, ability to move) confirms catatonia and supports ongoing benzodiazepine treatment. This is the recommended first-line approach in Australian psychiatry. (Source: RANZCP Guideline - Catatonia)

options.text: Defer pharmacological intervention and arrange brain MRI to exclude structural neurological cause
options.rationale: MRI is appropriate in atypical presentations but does not replace immediate symptomatic and diagnostic lorazepam challenge in clear-cut catatonia. The patient is at risk of dehydration, aspiration, and venous thromboembolism if left immobile. (Source: RANZCP Guideline - Catatonia)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is a syndrome of motor, behavioural, and affective disturbance characterised by features such as mutism, immobility, posturing, waxy flexibility, negativism, echolalia, and stereotypy. It complicates affective, psychotic, and medical illness and can be precipitated or worsened by antipsychotics. The lorazepam challenge (1 to 2 mg parenteral with response assessed at 30 to 60 minutes) is both diagnostic and first-line treatment. Refractory cases or malignant catatonia respond to electroconvulsive therapy. Antipsychotics are reduced or temporarily withdrawn until catatonia resolves.
key_points: Catatonia features include mutism, immobility, posturing, waxy flexibility, negativism
Lorazepam 1 to 2 mg challenge is first-line diagnostic and therapeutic
ECT is second-line, particularly for malignant catatonia or non-response
Antipsychotics can precipitate or worsen catatonia; consider holding during workup
explanation.pearls: Malignant catatonia (with autonomic instability, hyperthermia, rigidity) clinically overlaps with neuroleptic malignant syndrome but responds rapidly to electroconvulsive therapy with 80 to 100% remission rates.

stem: You are the emergency registrar reviewing a 28-year-old woman brought in 90 minutes after deliberate ingestion of approximately 50 amitriptyline 50 mg tablets. She is drowsy with GCS 11 but maintaining her airway. Her observations are concerning. She was charcoal-treated by paramedics 30 minutes after the ingestion. The poisons information centre advice has been sought and a plan formulated. An arterial blood gas and ECG have just been performed. The toxicology registrar is on the phone for consultation about further management. She has no past medical history.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 132 bpm, BP 92/56 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 96% room air, Temp 36.8 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Dilated pupils, dry mucous membranes, no clonus, no focal deficit
data_table.Investigations: pH 7.30, pCO2 32 mmHg, bicarbonate 18 mmol/L, lactate 3.4 mmol/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 3.8 mmol/L, ECG: sinus tachycardia 132 bpm with QRS 130 ms and R wave in aVR 4 mm

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Administer intravenous lipid emulsion 20% as bolus then infusion for lipid sink rescue
options.rationale: Intralipid is a second-line rescue therapy for refractory cardiovascular collapse not responding to bicarbonate. It is not first-line in haemodynamically borderline TCA overdose where bicarbonate is the priority. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology - Tricyclic Antidepressants)

options.text: Administer intravenous lidocaine 1 mg/kg bolus followed by an infusion for ventricular ectopy
options.rationale: Class IA and IC antiarrhythmics are contraindicated in TCA overdose because they share sodium channel blockade. Lidocaine has limited evidence and is not first-line. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology - Tricyclic Antidepressants)

options.text: Administer sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg intravenously and target a serum pH of 7.50 to 7.55
options.rationale: QRS prolongation over 100 ms and R wave in aVR over 3 mm are markers of severe sodium channel blockade in tricyclic overdose and predict arrhythmia and seizure. Hypertonic sodium bicarbonate is first-line, alkalinising serum to enhance dissociation from cardiac sodium channels and providing a sodium load. Target pH is 7.50 to 7.55. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology - Tricyclic Antidepressants)

options.text: Administer activated charcoal 50 g via nasogastric tube as second dose to improve drug binding
options.rationale: Single-dose charcoal within 1 to 2 hours has limited benefit and a further dose 90 minutes post-ingestion in a drowsy patient risks aspiration. The priority is sodium bicarbonate for the cardiotoxicity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology - Tricyclic Antidepressants)

options.text: Commence intravenous physostigmine 1 mg over 5 minutes to reverse anticholinergic toxicity
options.rationale: Physostigmine is contraindicated in TCA overdose because it can precipitate asystole and seizures. Anticholinergic features are managed supportively. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology - Tricyclic Antidepressants)

explanation.summary: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose causes sodium channel blockade, anticholinergic syndrome, alpha-adrenergic blockade, and central nervous system depression. ECG markers of severity include QRS over 100 ms (seizure and arrhythmia risk) and R wave in aVR over 3 mm. Sodium bicarbonate is the cornerstone treatment, working through both serum alkalinisation and sodium loading. Hypotension is treated with crystalloid and noradrenaline; benzodiazepines for seizures; intralipid for refractory cardiovascular collapse. Charcoal is reserved for early presentations with protected airway.
key_points: QRS over 100 ms or R wave in aVR over 3 mm predict arrhythmia and seizure
Sodium bicarbonate is first-line - alkalinise to pH 7.50 to 7.55
Class IA and IC antiarrhythmics and physostigmine are contraindicated
Intralipid 20% is rescue therapy for refractory cardiovascular collapse
explanation.pearls: An R wave over 3 mm in lead aVR within 6 hours of tricyclic overdose has a positive predictive value of approximately 81% for seizures or arrhythmias, making it a stronger predictor than QRS duration alone.

sources.label: Murray L, Daly F - Toxicology Handbook
